title: 25Q16JVNIM
content_raw:
# 25Q16JVNIM

## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/25Q16JVNIM

## System Layer
Hardware / Storage

## Summary
Winbond W25Q16JVXXX serial flash memory chip used in PS5. 16MB (128 Mbit) serial flash connected via SPI, storing boot-related data including the Secure Loader and NVS data.

## Key Concepts

### Pinout

| Pad | Internal Name | External Name | Type | Description |
|-----|---------------|---------------|------|-------------|
| 1 | /CS | /S50_SSB_SF_CS | | Chip Select Input |
| 2 | DO(IO1) | S50_SSB_SF_SIO1 | | Data Output (Data Input Output 1) |
| 3 | /WP(IO2) | 3.3V_SS_PG1 | | Write Protect Input (Data Input Output 2) |
| 4 | GND | GND | | Ground |
| 5 | DI(IO0) | S50_SSB_SF_SIO0 | | Data Input (Data Input Output 0) |
| 6 | CLK | S50G_SSB_SF_SCLK | | Serial Clock Input |
| 7 | /HOLD or /RESET(IO3) | 3.3V_SS_PG1 | | Hold or Reset Input (Data Input Output 3) |
| 8 | VCC | 3.3V_SS_PG1 | | Power Supply |

### Datasheet
- Winbond W25Q16JVXXX Datasheet
- Winbond W25Q16JVXX Datasheet

## System Role
The 25Q16JVNIM serial flash stores critical boot firmware including the Secure Loader IPL. It connects to the southbridge via SPI. Understanding its layout is essential for hardware-level analysis of the PS5 boot chain.

title: ACM Digital Library
content_raw:
# ACM Digital Library

## Source URL
https://dl.acm.org/

## Domain
dl.acm.org

## System Layer (choose one)
firmware

## Summary
The ACM Digital Library is a comprehensive collection of computing and information technology literature published by the Association for Computing Machinery. It includes journals, conference proceedings, magazines, newsletters, and books covering all areas of computer science including security, privacy, architecture, embedded systems, and cryptography. The library features proceedings from major conferences such as CCS (Computer and Communications Security), CODASPY (Data and Application Security and Privacy), and WiSec (Wireless and Mobile Network Security). ACM is transitioning to an Open Access publishing model.

## Key Concepts
- Computing research literature repository
- Peer-reviewed security and privacy publications
- Conference proceedings (CCS, CODASPY, WiSec)
- Journal articles on systems and software security
- Embedded systems and architecture research
- Open access publishing transition
- Security and privacy analytics research

## Security Relevance
Provides peer-reviewed research literature on computing security including firmware attacks, trusted execution environments, cryptographic protocols, and system security. ACM proceedings are a primary venue for security research disclosure and are relevant for understanding known vulnerabilities and mitigations in trusted systems.

## Relevance Tags
acm, research literature, security conferences, cryptography, computer security, published research, academic publications

title: AMD Platform Security Processor
content_raw:
# AMD Platform Security Processor
## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/AMD_Platform_Security_Processor
## System Layer
hardware
## Summary
The AMD Platform Security Processor (PSP), officially known as AMD Secure Technology, is the cryptoprocessor in charge of executing the PS5 boot ROM, handling crypto operations, secure key rings, and likely executes Secure Modules. Similar role to SAMU on PS4, CMeP on PS Vita, and Kirk on PSP.
## Key Concepts
- **Role**: Executes PS5 boot ROM, crypto operations, secure key rings
- **Function**: Manages boot process, initializes security mechanisms, monitors system for suspicious activity
- **Similar Processors**: SAMU (PS4), CMeP (PS Vita), Kirk (PSP)
- **External References**: UEFI PlugFest documentation, PSPReverse GitHub project
## System Role
Hardware security processor and trusted execution environment for the PS5.

title: AMD Secure Encrypted Virtualization
content_raw:
# AMD Secure Encrypted Virtualization

## Source URL
https://www.amd.com/en/developer/sev

## Domain
amd.com

## System Layer (choose one)
system_design

## Summary
AMD SEV (Secure Encrypted Virtualization) is a hardware memory encryption technology for AMD EPYC processors. SEV encrypts VM memory with per-VM keys transparent to the hypervisor. Successive generations added SEV-ES (Encrypted State) for register protection and SEV-SNP (Secure Nested Paging) for integrity and anti-replay protection against malicious hypervisors.

## Key Concepts
- SEV encrypts guest VM memory with AES-128/256 hardware encryption using dedicated on-die memory controller
- SEV-ES encrypts CPU register state when context-switching between guest and hypervisor
- SEV-SNP adds memory integrity protection via reverse map table, preventing hypervisor replay and remapping attacks
- Attestation via AMD Secure Processor (ASP) firmware-signed reports
- VM migration with encrypted state transfer between SEV-capable hosts
- Transparent to guest OS — no application code changes required

## Security Relevance
Provides the foundational hardware trust mechanism for confidential computing on AMD platforms. SEV's threat model assumes the hypervisor is untrusted — a critical property for trusted system design where cloud providers must be excluded from the guest's trusted computing base. Used by Google Cloud, Azure, and other confidential VM offerings.

## Relevance Tags
amd sev, secure encrypted virtualization, memory encryption, trusted execution, hardware security, snp, hypervisor isolation, confidential computing

title: AMD Secure Processor
content_raw:
# AMD Secure Processor

## Source URL
https://www.amd.com/en/technologies/secure-processor

## Domain
amd.com

## System Layer (choose one)
boot_chain

## Summary
The AMD Secure Processor (formerly Platform Security Processor or PSP) is a dedicated ARM Cortex-A5-based security subsystem integrated into AMD CPUs and APUs. It operates independently of the main x86 cores and runs its own firmware. It handles secure boot verification, cryptographic operations, Trusted Platform Module (fTPM) 2.0 functionality, and memory encryption (SME/SEV). The AMD Secure Processor verifies the signature of the UEFI BIOS before the x86 cores are released from reset, forming the hardware root of trust in AMD platforms. AMD fTPM is a firmware-based TPM implementation running within the Secure Processor.

## Key Concepts
- dedicated ARM Cortex-A5 security co-processor in AMD CPUs/APUs
- operates independently from main x86 cores
- verifies UEFI BIOS signature before x86 cores boot
- implements AMD fTPM 2.0 firmware-based TPM
- handles Secure Memory Encryption (SME) and Secure Encrypted Virtualization (SEV)
- runs proprietary firmware (AGESA)
- forms the hardware root of trust for AMD platforms
- provides secure boot chain verification

## Security Relevance
The AMD Secure Processor is the hardware root of trust in AMD-based systems. It enforces secure boot by verifying firmware signatures before the main CPU is released. Compromise of this component would break the entire boot chain trust model.

## Relevance Tags
amd, secure processor, psp, hardware root of trust, secure boot, ftpm, platform security, firmware verification

title: AMD Security
content_raw:
# AMD Security

## Source URL
https://www.amd.com/en/security

## Domain
amd.com

## System Layer (choose one)
firmware

## Summary
AMD's security portal provides information on platform security features including AMD Secure Processor (AMD-SP), Secure Encrypted Virtualization (SEV), Secure Memory Encryption (SME), and Platform Secure Boot. The AMD Secure Processor is a dedicated ARM Cortex-A5-based security processor that manages boot, encryption keys, and attestation. AMD SEV provides encrypted VM memory for confidential computing. The portal also publishes security advisory bulletins, vulnerability disclosures, and mitigation guidance for AMD products.

## Key Concepts
- AMD Secure Processor (AMD-SP) architecture
- Secure Encrypted Virtualization (SEV, SEV-ES, SEV-SNP)
- Secure Memory Encryption (SME)
- Platform Secure Boot implementation
- AMD firmware Trusted Platform Module (fTPM)
- AMD Platform Security Processor (PSP)
- Confidential computing with encrypted VM memory
- Security advisory and vulnerability disclosure program

## Security Relevance
AMD's firmware security architecture defines the root of trust for AMD-based systems. The AMD-SP/PSP operates as an independent security processor that initializes the system and manages encryption keys, making it the central trust anchor. SEV/SEV-SNP provides confidential computing guarantees. Vulnerabilities in AMD-SP firmware (e.g., CROSSLINE, voltage glitching attacks) directly compromise platform trust.

## Relevance Tags
amd, secure processor, psp, sev, sev-snp, platform security, secure boot, confidential computing

title: ARM Trusted Firmware-A
content_raw:
# ARM Trusted Firmware-A

## Source URL
https://github.com/ARM-software/arm-trusted-firmware

## Domain
github.com

## System Layer (choose one)
firmware

## Summary
Trusted Firmware-A (TF-A) is a reference implementation of secure world software for Arm A-Profile architectures (Armv8-A and Armv7-A). It includes an Exception Level 3 (EL3) Secure Monitor and provides a starting point for productization of secure world boot and runtime firmware in AArch32 or AArch64 execution states. TF-A implements Arm interface standards including PSCI, TBBR-CLIENT, SMC Calling Convention, SCMI, and SDEI. The repository has over 19,500 commits, 2,200 stars, and is written primarily in C (91%) with Assembly, Makefile, and Python components.

## Key Concepts
- EL3 Secure Monitor implementation
- Power State Coordination Interface (PSCI)
- Trusted Board Boot Requirements (TBBR-CLIENT)
- SMC Calling Convention standard
- System Control and Management Interface (SCMI)
- Software Delegated Exception Interface (SDEI)
- Portable secure world firmware across Arm platforms
- AArch32 and AArch64 execution state support

## Security Relevance
Implements the highest privilege level (EL3) firmware for Arm processors, which serves as the root of trust for the entire platform. The secure monitor handles transitions between normal and secure worlds, and the trusted boot requirements ensure integrity of the boot chain from the first stage.

## Relevance Tags
arm, trusted firmware, el3, secure monitor, psci, tbbr, secure boot, aarch64

title: arXiv
content_raw:
# arXiv

## Source URL
https://arxiv.org/

## Domain
arxiv.org

## System Layer (choose one)
system_design

## Summary
arXiv is a free, open-access e-print archive hosted by Cornell University containing nearly 2.4 million scholarly articles. Covers physics, mathematics, computer science, quantitative biology, quantitative finance, statistics, electrical engineering, systems science, and economics. Computer Science repository (CoRR) includes cryptography, security, operating systems, and systems design.

## Key Concepts
- Open-access preprint server — no peer review requirement for submission
- Computer Science (CoRR) categories include Cryptography and Security (cs.CR), Operating Systems (cs.OS), Hardware Architecture (cs.AR)
- Rapid dissemination of research before formal peer review and publication
- Version tracking with submission timestamps
- Moderation system ensures topical relevance without full peer review
- Supported by Simons Foundation, member institutions, and donors

## Security Relevance
Early-access repository for cutting-edge trusted computing research before formal publication. Frequently the first place where TEE vulnerabilities, secure boot attacks, and new trusted system designs are publicly disclosed. Essential for monitoring latest developments in systems security research.

## Relevance Tags
arxiv, open access, preprint, research, computer science, security, cryptography, trusted computing

title: arXiv.org e-Print Archive
content_raw:
# arXiv.org e-Print Archive

## Source URL
https://arxiv.org/

## Domain
arxiv.org

## System Layer (choose one)
boot_chain

## Summary
arXiv is a free distribution service and open-access archive for nearly 2.4 million scholarly articles in physics, mathematics, computer science, quantitative biology, quantitative finance, statistics, electrical engineering and systems science, and economics. Materials are not peer-reviewed by arXiv (moderation only). The Computer Science section includes Cryptography and Security (cs.CR), Hardware Architecture (cs.AR), and Operating Systems (cs.OS) categories relevant to boot chain research. arXiv provides rapid dissemination of preprints in secure boot, trusted computing, firmware security, and TPM analysis before or alongside formal peer review.

## Key Concepts
- free open-access preprint repository (Cornell University)
- ~2.4 million scholarly articles
- Computer Science section includes cs.CR (Cryptography and Security)
- relevant categories: cs.CR, cs.AR, cs.OS, cs.SE
- rapid dissemination of research before/during peer review
- not peer-reviewed (moderated for relevance only)
- supports search by author, title, abstract, and full text
- PDF and TeX source access for all papers
- funded by Simons Foundation, member institutions, and donations

## Security Relevance
arXiv is a major source for early/pre-publication research on boot chain vulnerabilities, secure boot implementations, TPM attacks, and firmware security. Many foundational papers appear on arXiv before conference publication.

## Relevance Tags
arxiv, preprint, open access, research, computer science, cryptography, security, boot chain, trusted computing

title: Auth IDs
content_raw:
# Auth IDs

## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Auth_IDs

## System Layer
Security / System Software

## Summary
Lists Authentication IDs (Auth IDs) used by PS5 system software to identify and authorize different system components, kernel modules, and applications.

## Key Concepts

### Main Auth IDs
- `4100000000000001` -> Secure Kernel
- `4100000000000002` -> Kernel
- `4100000000001001` -> ???
- `4100000000001002` -> ???
- `4100000000001101` -> ???
- `4100000000001102` -> ???

### System/System_Ex Auth IDs
- `4800000000000024` -> /system/common/lib/ScePlayReady.self
- `480000000000003b` -> /system/common/lib/ScePlayReady2.self
- `4800000000010001` -> /system/sys/set_upper.self
- `4800000010000010` -> /system/vsh/app/NPXS40109/webrtc_daemon.self
- `4900000000000002` -> Shared by many system libraries (.sprx files) in /system/common/lib/, /system/priv/lib/, /system_ex/app/, /system_ex/common_ex/lib/, /system_ex/priv_ex/lib/
- `4800000000001006` -> /system/vsh/app/NPXS40038/eboot.bin (and many other VSH app eboot.bin files)
- `4800000000010003` -> ./sys/decid.elf
- `4800000000000005` -> ./sys/orbis_audiod.elf
- `4800000000000007` -> ./sys/SceSysCore.elf
- `4800000000000010` -> ./vsh/SceShellCore.elf
- `4800000010000001` -> /system_ex/app/NPXS40140/BdmvPlayerCore.elf
- `4800000010000005` -> /system_ex/app/NPXS40140/cdc/bin/bdj.elf

### GnmDriver Auth
- `4900000000000007` -> /system/common/lib/libSceGnmDriver.sprx
- `4900000000000007` -> /system/common/lib/libSceGnmDriverCompat1.sprx
- `4900000000000007` -> /system/common/lib/libSceGnmDriverForNeoMode.sprx

### BD Player Auth
- `4900000000000003` -> /system_ex/app/NPXS40140/libAacs.sprx
- `4900000000000006` -> /system_ex/app/NPXS40140/libBdplus.sprx

## System Role
Auth IDs are used by the SceSbl (Secure Boot Loader) authentication system to verify and authorize module loading. The ID prefix indicates privilege level: 41 = kernel, 48 = system process, 49 = system library. Auth ID 4900000000000002 is the most common, used by the vast majority of system libraries.

- `4900000000000003` -> /system_ex/app/NPXS40140/libAacs.sprx
- `4900000000000006` -> /system_ex/app/NPXS40140/libBdplus.sprx

## System Role
Auth IDs are used by the SceSbl (Secure Boot Loader) authentication system to verify and authorize module loading. The ID prefix indicates privilege level: 41 = kernel, 48 = system process, 49 = system library. Auth ID 4900000000000002 is the most common, used by the vast majority of system libraries.

title: AWS Nitro System
content_raw:
# AWS Nitro System

## Source URL
https://aws.amazon.com/ec2/nitro/

## Domain
aws.amazon.com

## System Layer (choose one)
system_design

## Summary
AWS Nitro System is the virtualization infrastructure for EC2 instances. Composed of dedicated Nitro Cards (offloading VPC, EBS, instance storage), Nitro Security Chip, a lightweight Nitro Hypervisor, and Nitro Enclaves for isolated compute environments. Delivers near bare-metal performance by offloading virtualization functions to dedicated hardware.

## Key Concepts
- Nitro Cards offload and accelerate network (VPC), storage (EBS), and I/O functions
- Nitro Security Chip locks down administrative access (including Amazon employees)
- Nitro Hypervisor provides CPU/memory isolation with near bare-metal performance
- Nitro Enclaves create isolated compute environments for sensitive data processing
- NitroTPM provides TPM 2.0 functionality for attestation and key storage
- Isolation Engine enforces mathematical guarantees of instance separation
- Supports bare metal instances allowing customer-owned hypervisors

## Security Relevance
Represents a major commercial hypervisor security architecture. Nitro's dedicated hardware approach minimizes attack surface by removing software virtualization layers. The locked-down administrative model (no human access) is a significant trust architecture pattern applicable to any system where the platform owner must be excluded from the trusted computing base.

## Relevance Tags
aws, nitro, hypervisor, hardware isolation, virtualization security, trusted execution, enclave, tpm

title: Backup And Restore
content_raw:
# Backup And Restore

## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Backup_And_Restore

## System Layer
System Software

## Summary
The Backup And Restore (BAR) utility allows PS5 users to back up and restore user data (settings, saved data, screenshots, video clips, games, patches) to/from an external USB drive.

## Key Concepts
- Creates `archive.dat` files on USB drive containing backup of built-in storage data
- Not present on systems with FW 1.02 and older that never connected to PSN
- Present at least since FW 2.10 (2020-11-06)
- Uses PFS (PlayStation File System) encryption for backup data
- Related kernel functions: `sceSblBarCreateContext`, `sceSblBarUpdateAad`, `sceSblBarUpdateDecrypt`, `sceSblBarUpdateEncrypt`, `sceSblBarFinishDecrypt`, `sceSblBarFinishEncrypt`

## System Role
The BAR system uses SceSbl encryption functions to create secure encrypted backups. Understanding the archive.dat format and associated crypto operations is relevant for data recovery and forensic analysis.

title: Backwards compatibility
content_raw:
# Backwards compatibility
## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Backwards_compatibility
## System Layer
system_software
## Summary
List of PS4 titles tested on PS5 with compatibility ratings: Incompatible, In-Game, Playable, Perfect, Enhanced. The majority of tested titles run at Perfect quality with no issues.
## Key Concepts
- Incompatible: game does NOT boot
- In-Game: game boots but may not be playable
- Playable: game boots with possible minor issues
- Perfect: no perceived issues
- Enhanced: specific PS5 patches/enhancements
- 40+ tested titles documented
- P.T. is intentionally blocked by Konami
- God of War (2018) Enhanced: 4K/60fps with potential stability issues
- Most titles run Perfect
## System Role
PS4 backward compatibility reference

title: BD-JB: Blu-ray Disc Java Sandbox Escape
content_raw:
# BD-JB: Blu-ray Disc Java Sandbox Escape

## Source URL
https://github.com/TheOfficialFloW/bd-jb

## Domain
github.com

## System Layer
jailbreaking

## Summary
BD-JB by TheFloW (Andy Nguyen) is a PS5/PS4 exploit that chains 5 vulnerabilities in the Blu-ray Java (BD-J) stack to achieve arbitrary code execution. Presented at HardWear.io 2022 and Hexacon 2022. It is firmware-agnostic, uses ROP-less code execution via JIT, and works on PS5 up to 7.61 (patched in 8.00). Initial PS5 support covered up to 4.51, extended to 7.61 via a path traversal flaw. Requires a Blu-ray disc drive and burned BD-R/BD-RE media.

## Key Concepts
- Blu-ray Java (BD-J) sandbox escape
- ROP-less code execution via JIT compiler
- Insecure deserialization in UserPreferenceManagerImpl
- Privileged constructor and method invocation
- Path traversal for extended firmware coverage
- Payload loading on port 9019 (original) or 9025 (JAR loader)
- PS5 XOM (eXecute-Only Memory) bypass — prevents reading .text segment yet code execution achieved
- Kernel API (kread/kwrite) integrated in 2024 updates
- Partial reimplementation by Sleirsgoevy without kernel component
- 2x performance optimization in 2024 revision

## Security Relevance
Demonstrates multi-vulnerability chaining across Java sandbox restrictions, deserialization, and JIT internals. Bypasses XOM (eXecute-Only Memory), a hardware-level code protection mechanism, showing that code-execution mitigations alone are insufficient without address-space layout randomization and integrity checks in the BD-J runtime.

## Relevance Tags
ps5, ps4, jailbreak, exploit, bluray, bd-j, java, sandbox-escape, jit, xom, theflow

title: Black Hat Archives
content_raw:
# Black Hat Archives

## Source URL
https://www.blackhat.com/html/archives.html

## Domain
blackhat.com

## System Layer (choose one)
firmware

## Summary
Black Hat is a premier information security conference series featuring briefings on cutting-edge security research including firmware exploitation, hardware attacks, boot security, and trusted platform vulnerabilities. The archives contain presentation materials and whitepapers from past conferences covering topics such as UEFI exploits, Intel ME/AMD PSP attacks, TPM bypass techniques, secure boot bypasses, and console jailbreak research. Presentations are categorized by year and region (USA, Europe, Asia).

## Key Concepts
- Firmware and hardware exploitation presentations
- UEFI and BIOS attack techniques
- Intel ME and AMD PSP security research
- TPM and secure element attack vectors
- Secure boot and measured boot bypasses
- Console and embedded system exploitation
- Boot ROM and early initialization attacks

## Security Relevance
Archives serve as a repository of disclosed firmware and hardware vulnerabilities affecting the trusted computing base. Black Hat presentations frequently disclose novel attack techniques against platform firmware, boot chains, and hardware security mechanisms, directly informing trust model analysis.

## Relevance Tags
black hat, firmware exploitation, hardware hacking, uefi, boot security, conference archives, offensive research

title: Bond
content_raw:
# Bond
## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Bond
## System Layer
hardware
## Summary
Codename of the DualSense controller. Firmware is stored under /system_ex/etc/. Contains Banana (main), Venom (audio), and Betty firmware components.
## Key Concepts
- **Bond**: Codename for DualSense
- **Firmware Location**: /system_ex/etc/ on PS5
- **Banana Firmware Structure** (offset/size/description):
  - 0x00/0x40: Copyright String
  - 0x40/0x10: Build Date String
  - 0x50/0x10: Build Hour String
  - 0x62/0x2: Product ID (e.g., 0xCE6)
  - 0x68/0x4: Full Size
  - 0x6C/0x4: SDK Version
  - 0x70/0x4: Firmware Version
  - 0x74/0x4: SwSeries
  - 0x78/0x4: ControllerVersion
- **Venom Firmware Structure**: Type string "VenomB", build date, firmware version, body size
- **Betty Firmware Structure**: Type string "BettyBND", build date, body size
- **Example Versions**: Nov 2020 (SwSeries 0x0004, ControllerVersion 0x0210, FW 2.50)
## System Role
DualSense controller firmware structure documentation.

title: BootLogo
content_raw:
# BootLogo

## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/BootLogo

## System Layer
System Software

## Summary
The PS5 BootLogo is displayed by SceSysAvControl and is a 256x256 "PS Logo" image, BPE encoded and embedded in the SceSysAvControl module.

## Key Concepts
- Module location: /SceSysAvControl.elf
- Image size: 256x256 pixels
- Encoding: BPE (Bit Pack Encoding) with some filter
- Search signature: `2A 80 80 07` (magic bytes for BPE encoded data, no header)
- Note: The signature may change in future firmware

## System Role
The boot logo is part of the early system initialization display chain, rendered by SceSysAvControl (Audio-Visual Control) during startup before the main UI loads.

title: Bugs
content_raw:
# Bugs
## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Bugs
## System Layer
system_software
## Summary
Catalog of PS5 bugs organized by exploitability. Covers WebKit JSC bugs (get_by_id_with_this ProxyObject JSScope leak CVE, integer underflow CVE-2023-38600, mmap alignment issue), JIT-disabled bugs (CVE-2024-27833 SBFX overflow, CVE-2023-41993 clobberize, DFG Abstract Interpreter type confusion), non-exploitable memory exhaustion bugs (CVE-2023-28205 CloneDeserializer UaF, heap/string overflow). Also covers Chrome V8 CVEs (2025-6554, 2025-5419, 2024-0517) and Mozilla SpiderMonkey (CVE-2018-5093 WebAssembly Table underflow), all untested on PS5.
## Key Concepts
- WebKit JSC bugs organized by exploitability status
- get_by_id_with_this + ProxyObject: JSScope object leak, FW 6.00-9.60, patched FW 10.00
- CVE-2023-38600: integer underflow in genericTypedArrayViewProtoFuncCopyWithin, FW 6.00-8.60, patched FW 9.00
- mmap pointer address misalignment: HAVE_MAP_ALIGNED workaround in OptionsPlaystation.cmake
- CVE-2024-27833: JSC SBFX immediate overflow (JIT disabled on PS5, not exploitable)
- CVE-2023-41993: JSC DFG clobberize ByOffset nodes (JIT disabled, not exploitable)
- DFG Abstract Interpreter clobberWorld type confusion (JIT disabled, not exploitable)
- CVE-2023-28205: CloneDeserializer deserialize UaF, FW <= 7.61, crash only
- Chrome V8 CVEs: 2025-6554, 2025-5419, 2024-0517 — untested on PS5
- CVE-2025-6558: requires WebGL2, probably not affecting PS5
- CVE-2018-5093: SpiderMonkey WebAssembly Table integer underflow, untested
## System Role
Bug tracking for potential exploit development

title: Build Strings
content_raw:
# Build Strings
## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Build_Strings
## System Layer
System Software / Build System
## Summary
Documentation of PS5 firmware build strings extracted from kernel memory dumps and system logs, covering CP Box EMC, EAP KBL, Secure Loader, VBIOS, Kernel, and index.dat from SceShellCore across retail, testkit, and devkit firmware versions.
## Key Concepts
- **CP Box EMC**: TestKit version 1.0.1.0
- **CP Box EAP KBL**: EAP SDK versions from 5.501.000 (2019-2021), with Sycorax build strings (0.5.7.0 to 2.5.5.3)
- **Secure Loader**: Build string format contains "sys-revision" and "sys-repository-path" (e.g., "Oberon-KDE 2021/04/06 05:22 releases/03.00 171343 pprbld-w54.build.rd.scei.sony.co.jp")
- **VBIOS**: AMD ATOMBIOSBK strings (e.g., "AMDObrGeneri.2132099 .518911 . 06/15/20,23:33:14")
- **Kernel**: Build strings from sysctl kern.version (e.g., "r153000/releases/01.00 May 21 2020 05:17:55"), also contains equivalent PS4 SDK version
- **Index.dat from SceShellCore**: Output on boot (5-6s) on TestKit/DevKit/QA/UART-enabled CEX, contains release, build type, security/sys/middleware/vsh repository paths and revisions
- Key entries: UPD-version, release (hex), build (cex/testkit/devkit), security-revision, sys-revision, sdk-internal-build-number, middleware-revision, vsh-revision, Framework-Version
## System Role
Provides detailed build identification data for forensic analysis of PS5 firmware provenance and version tracking.

title: Button Combos
content_raw:
# Button Combos
## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Button_Combos
## System Layer
System Software / Shell
## Summary
Button combinations that trigger special system menus and debug functions on PS5.
## Key Concepts
- **Safe Mode**: While console is off, hold power button until two beeps.
- **More System Information**: In Settings > System > System Software > Console Information, hold L1 + L3 + Triangle for 5 seconds, then release and press D-Pad Up + Options.
- **Staff Mode (IDU)**: Hold L1 + L2, then press Circle, Cross, Square, Triangle, D-Pad Right in sequence, then release L1 + L2.
- **PsnInGameCommerce**: In debug settings, press D-Pad Left + Square + R1 simultaneously.
- **Toggle Extra Debug Menu**: On main menu, press Start + L3 simultaneously (requires SblRcMgrIsAllowDebugMenuForSettings enabled).
- **Crash The Shell**: With registry option enabled, various 4-button combos involving D-Pad Up + L1 + R1 + another button.
## System Role
Provides hidden diagnostic and debug system access via controller input sequences.

content_raw:
# Codenames
## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Codenames
## System Layer
hardware
## Summary
PS5 hardware and firmware codenames organized by themes: James Bond movies, William Shakespeare works, Islands, and Lord of the Rings.
## Key Concepts
- **James Bond Movies**: Bond (DualSense), Aston (early DualSense), Lotus (Media Remote), Walther (Trigger System), Venom (Audio Codec), Tahoe (first Southbridge rev), Sierra (second Southbridge rev)
- **William Shakespeare**: Prospero (PS5), Caliban (CP Box EAP Kernel), Sycorax (CP Box KBL), Setebos (CP Box EMC FW), Oberon/Oberon Plus (CPU part of APU), Viola (PS5 Pro APU), Titania (EFC/EAP), Ariel (GPU part of APU), Tempest 3D Audio
- **Islands**: Salina (first Southbridge rev/EMC)
- **Lord of the Rings**: Antonio (prototype DevKit board), Tirion (SysCon on DevKit proto), Onion (DSP firmware)
- **Others**: Sano (prototype DevKit motherboard), Carlo (CP board proto), Floyd (TPM chip), Azalia (GPU part), Gaikai (Remote Play)
- **Firmware Hex Codenames**: 40000001-40000003 (Syscon), 40030001 (cp_fw), C0000001 (emc_ipl), C0030002 (wlanbt_fw), C0040001-C0040002 (floyd_fw), C0050001-C0050002 (usbc_fw), C3000005-C300000B (titania_fw)
## System Role
Codename reference for PS5 hardware components and firmware modules.

title: Confidential Computing Consortium
content_raw:
# Confidential Computing Consortium

## Source URL
https://confidentialcomputing.io/

## Domain
confidentialcomputing.io

## System Layer (choose one)
system_design

## Summary
Linux Foundation project focused on securing data in use through confidential computing technologies. Members include AMD, Google, Intel, Microsoft, Meta, NVIDIA, and others. Produces white papers, common terminology definitions, compliance frameworks, and open-source projects for trusted execution environments (TEEs) and attestation.

## Key Concepts
- Confidential computing encrypts data in use (during processing), complementing encryption at rest and in transit
- Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) isolate code and data from host OS and cloud provider
- Attestation mechanisms verify TEE integrity to remote parties
- Open-source projects include Verdict, COCONUT-SVSM, and trusted firmware
- Common Terminology for Confidential Computing standardizes industry definitions
- Governance, Risk, and Compliance (GRC) working group for regulatory frameworks

## Security Relevance
Defines the industry architecture for protecting data during computation, critical for trusted cloud models. TEE-based confidential computing is a key mechanism for reducing trust requirements in multi-tenant systems by providing hardware-enforced isolation from the platform owner.

## Relevance Tags
confidential computing, trusted execution environment, tee, attestation, data in use, hardware isolation, linux foundation, enclave

title: CP Box
content_raw:
# CP Box
## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/CP_Box
## System Layer
debugging
## Summary
The CP Box (model CPBH-100) is a test/debug device connecting to a PS5 TestKit via USB-C. It has Engineering and Normal modes, provides Ethernet connectivity (DEV LAN), and controls debug mode. Without a CP Box, TestKits boot in Release Mode. With CP Box, Assist Mode is available. Not hot-pluggable; must be connected before power-on.
## Key Concepts
- Model CPBH-100
- Two modes: Engineering Mode and Normal Mode
- Engineering Mode: CP Box powered, USB-C to PS5 only
- Normal Mode: USB-C to portable HDD + Ethernet to network + USB-C to PS5
- DEV LAN: Ethernet port connects to host computer
- CP Box status LEDs: CP INIT, NETWORK INIT, SPEED, LINK/ACT, STATUS
- Without CP Box: TestKit boots in Release Mode
- With CP Box: TestKit can switch to Assist Mode
- Assist Mode persists in memory even when powered off
- PS5 power-on checks for CP Box; shows error if hot-plugged
- CP Box can read PS5 info (serial, mode) even when PS5 is shut down
- Prototype CPB-K01: CXD90046GG main chip, 2x CP systems (recovery + normal)
- VR port (USB-C) activates for PS VR2 after cpupdate ver 2700
## System Role
TestKit debug interface and mode control hardware

title: CROSSLINE: Breaking "Security-by-Crash" based Memory Isolation in AMD SEV
content_raw:
# CROSSLINE: Breaking "Security-by-Crash" based Memory Isolation in AMD SEV

## Source URL
https://arxiv.org/abs/2008.00146

## Domain
arxiv.org

## System Layer (choose one)
firmware

## Summary
This paper (Li, Zhang, Lin, 2020) presents CROSSLINE attacks against AMD Secure Encrypted Virtualization (SEV). The attack exploits SEV's improper use of Address Space Identifiers (ASIDs) for controlling VM access to encrypted memory pages, cache lines, and TLB entries. An attacker VM can change its ASID to impersonate a victim VM. CROSSLINE V1 decrypts victim page tables; CROSSLINE V2 constructs encryption/decryption oracles. Successfully demonstrated on SEV and SEV-ES processors.

## Key Concepts
- AMD SEV memory isolation vulnerability
- ASID reuse and impersonation attack
- Encrypted memory page decryption
- Cache line and TLB side-channel exploitation
- Security-by-crash design failure
- Cross-VM confidential computing breach
- SEV and SEV-ES demonstrated exploitation

## Security Relevance
Directly demonstrates a critical failure in AMD SEV's firmware-enforced memory isolation, which is designed to protect confidential computing workloads from untrusted hypervisors. The attack breaks the trust boundary between virtual machines that SEV firmware is supposed to enforce, compromising the confidentiality guarantees of the platform.

## Relevance Tags
amd, sev, memory isolation, side channel, confidential computing, virtualization security, asid

title: CXD90060GG
content_raw:
# CXD90060GG
## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/CXD90060GG
## System Layer
hardware
## Summary
SIE CXD90060GG APU chip. RDNA2 2.23GHz. Codename: Oberon. Includes GDDR6 memory interface (channels E0, E1, D0, D1), SVI2 power management, and extensive pinout for 1.0V SOC power domains.
## Key Concepts
- **Function**: APU (Accelerated Processing Unit)
- **Codename**: Oberon
- **GPU**: RDNA2 2.23GHz
- **Memory Interface**: GDDR6, 4 channels (E0, E1, D0, D1) with DQ, DBI, EDC, WCK signals
- **Power Domains**: VDDCR_GFX (1.0V_SOC_VGFX), VDDCR_CORE (1.0V_SOC_VCORE)
- **Memory Voltage**: 1.35V_G6_VMEMIO
- **Control Interfaces**: SVI2 (SVD0, SVC0, SVT0) for voltage regulation
- **Large BGA package with hundreds of pins for power delivery and signal routing**
## System Role
Main APU (CPU + GPU) for the PlayStation 5.

title: CXD90061GG
content_raw:
# CXD90061GG
## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/CXD90061GG
## System Layer
hardware
## Summary
SIE CXD90061GG EMC (Embedded Micro Controller) with SysCon bundled. MediaTek 3613 chip. Codename: Salina. Provides southbridge functions including ethernet, SATA, USB, PCIe, I2C, GPIO, PWM, and system management.
## Key Concepts
- **Function**: EMC (Embedded Micro Controller) with SysCon
- **Codename**: Salina
- **Base Chip**: MediaTek 3613
- **Peripherals**:
  - Gigabit Ethernet (4 pairs MDI0-MDI3)
  - SATA (Port 0 and 1)
  - USB 2.0 (Port 2)
  - PCIe Gen3 (to Flash Controller), PCIe Gen1 (to WiFi)
  - I2C (HDMI, SOC, general purpose)
  - SPI (Flash)
  - UART (debug)
  - PWM (FAN, LED, Buzzer)
  - GPIO
- **System Management**: AUXADC (thermistor monitoring, power button, AC detect), RTC (32.768kHz crystal)
- **Power Supplies**: 3.3V, 1.8V, 1.15V, 1.2V domains
- **Clock Generation**: 25MHz main crystal, 27MHz for HDMI (FLAVA), 100MHz PCIe reference clocks
- **Key Signals**: PSW_* (power sequencing), /SOC_ALERT (thermal trip), /SOC_THERMTRIP, /HDMI_IRQ, FAN_PWM
## System Role
Southbridge/EMC controller for PS5 providing peripheral connectivity and system management.

title: CXD90062GG
content_raw:
# CXD90062GG
## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/CXD90062GG
## System Layer
hardware
## Summary
SIE CXD90062GG NVMe SSD controller based on Marvell 88SS1098. Codename: Zao. Controls 12 NAND channels (CH4-CH11) with PCIe Gen4 interface for M.2 SSD expansion.
## Key Concepts
- **Function**: NVMe SSD Controller (Flash Controller)
- **Codename**: Zao
- **Base Design**: Marvell 88SS1098 NVMe SSD controller
- **NAND Channels**: 12 channels (CH4 through CH11) with 8-bit data bus each
- **Channel Signals**: AD[0:7] (data), DQS/DQSB (data strobe), RE/REB (read enable), WE (write enable), CLE (command latch), ALE (address latch), CE0-CE3 (chip enable)
- **PCIe Interface**: M.2 slot with 4 lanes (RC1_0 through RC1_3)
- **Power**: 1.2V_FC_VDDQFIO (NAND I/O), various power/ground planes
- **NAND Interface Voltage**: 1.2V VDDQFIO
## System Role
Internal NVMe SSD controller for PS5 flash storage and M.2 expansion slot interface.

title: Devices
content_raw:
# Devices
## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Devices
## System Layer
kernel
## Summary
Comprehensive listing of PS5 kernel devices accessible through IOCTL. Covers Platform Security Processor (PSP/MP0), System Management Unit (SMU/MP1), Trusted Execution Environment (TEE/MP3), A53 co-processor (MP4), and detailed IOCTL command tables for each device subsystem.
## Key Concepts
- /dev/ devices accessible via IOCTL syscalls from kernel
- MP0 (PSP): AMD Platform Security Processor - security functions
- MP1 (SMU): Xtensa CPU for power/clock/thermal management
- MP2 (SFP): Sensor Fusion Processor (not present on PS5)
- MP3 (TEE): Trusted Execution Environment on PSP, PlayReady SL3000 DRM
- MP4 (A53): ARM Cortex-A53 co-processor for I/O and memory management
- Over 100 kernel device entries documented
- /dev/bar: Backup and Restore for shellcore
- /dev/duid: Disc Unique ID
- /dev/dldbg: Dynamic Library Debug
- /dev/fttrm: Film/TV Tracking Rights Management (Blu-ray DRM)
- /dev/icc_floyd: TPM (Trusted Platform Module)
- /dev/manuauth: Manufacturer authorization
- /dev/nsfsctl: Namespace Filesystem Control
- /dev/pfsctldev: PlayStation FileSystem Control
- /dev/pfsmgr: PFS Manager (trophies, savedata, keystone)
- /dev/pup_update0: Firmware update device
- /dev/rootparam: Root PARAM.SFO/JSON verification
- /dev/sflash0: Serial Flash access (2MB)
- /dev/wlanbt: Wireless LAN + Bluetooth
## System Role
Kernel device node reference for PS5 IOCTL interface

title: DualSense
content_raw:
# DualSense
## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/DualSense
## System Layer
hardware
## Summary
The DualSense wireless controller for PS5 features haptic feedback, adaptive triggers, built-in microphone, USB Type-C, Bluetooth 2.402-2.48 GHz, and a 1,560 mAh battery. Codename: Bond.
## Key Concepts
- **Codenames**: Bond (DualSense), Spider (Main MCU/DSP), Venom (Audio Codec), Onion (Sub DSP), Walther (Triggers Assembly)
- **Main MCU/DSP**: SIE CXD9006GG (rebranded MediaTek MT3616XXX, ARM Cortex M4 & N9 DSP Core)
- **PMIC**: Dialog DA9087
- **Audio Codec**: Realtek ALC5524 (Venom)
- **Audio Amplifier**: Realtek ALC1016 or Nuvoton NAU8225 3.0W Class-D
- **Main Firmware**: Runs on ARM Cortex M4
- **Revision IDs**: 0x0CE6 (ModelType.BOND), 0x0CE7 (ModelType.ASTON), 0x0D5A (ModelType.LOTUS)
- **USB VID/PID**: 054c:0ce6 Sony Corp.
- **Wireless Interface**: Bluetooth 2.402-2.48 GHz, 2.5 mW power output
- **Wired Interface**: USB Type-C
- **Battery Capacity**: 1,560 mAh
- **USB Classes**: Audio (speaker, microphone), HID
- **Board Revisions**: BDM-010 (retail), BDM-010 R10 (engineering sample with MT3616ECT)
## System Role
Primary input controller for the PlayStation 5 console.

- **Revision IDs**: 0x0CE6 (ModelType.BOND), 0x0CE7 (ModelType.ASTON), 0x0D5A (ModelType.LOTUS)
- **USB VID/PID**: 054c:0ce6 Sony Corp.
- **Wireless Interface**: Bluetooth 2.402-2.48 GHz, 2.5 mW power output
- **Wired Interface**: USB Type-C
- **Battery Capacity**: 1,560 mAh
- **USB Classes**: Audio (speaker, microphone), HID
- **Board Revisions**: BDM-010 (retail), BDM-010 R10 (engineering sample with MT3616ECT)
## System Role
Primary input controller for the PlayStation 5 console.

title: DualSense DFU Modes
content_raw:
# DualSense DFU Modes
## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/DualSense_DFU_Modes
## System Layer
hardware
## Summary
Three DFU modes exist for DualSense firmware flashing: Primary Boot Loader (PBL), Secondary Boot Loader (SBL), and Main Mode.
## Key Concepts
- **DFU Primary Boot Loader Mode**: Used to flash only the secondary boot loader (SBL)
- **DFU Secondary Boot Loader Mode**: Used to flash Main Firmware (Banana), Venom Firmware (Audio), and Onion Firmware (DSP)
- **Main Mode**: Used to flash Venom Firmware (Audio), Onion Firmware (DSP), and Bluetooth Patches
## System Role
Firmware update modes for the DualSense wireless controller.

title: DualSense HID Commands
content_raw:
# DualSense HID Commands
## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/DualSense_HID_Commands
## System Layer
hardware
## Summary
Comprehensive list of HID commands for the DualSense controller, organized by ReportID, DeviceID, and ActionID.
## Key Concepts
- **Get MCU Unique ID**: ReportID=128, DeviceID=1, ActionID=9
- **Read Device Info**: ReportID=128, DeviceID=1, ActionID=12
- **Erase Device Info**: ReportID=128, DeviceID=1, ActionID=13 (DANGER)
- **Reset**: ReportID=128, DeviceID=1, ActionID=1
- **Reboot Secure Boot Loader**: ReportID=128, RebootCmd=1
- **Get Firmware Info**: ReportID=32
- **Get BT Address**: ReportID=129, DeviceID=9, ActionID=2, DataLen=6
- **Set BT Address**: ReportID=128, DeviceID=9, ActionID=1, ReadCmd=2, DataLen=6
- **Set DFU Mode**: ReportID=160, DisableMode=0, EnablePBLMode=1, EnableSBLMode=2, EnableMode=1
- **Flash**: ReportID=240, DownloadCmd=0, DataLen=57, WriteCmd=1
- **NVS Lock**: ReportID=128, DeviceID=3, ActionID=1
- **NVS Unlock**: ReportID=128, DeviceID=3, ActionID=2
- **NVS Lock Status**: ReportID=128, DeviceID=3, ActionID=3 (Results: 0=Unlocked, 1=Locked)
- **Device IDs**: 1=Main MCU, 2=Power, 3=NVS, 5=Touch Panel, 6=Venom FW, 9=BT, 14=BT Patch, 15=Venom, 16=Spider DSP, 17=VDD External
## System Role
HID command interface for reverse engineering and firmware management of the DualSense controller.

title: EMC
content_raw:
# EMC
## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/EMC
## System Layer
security
## Summary
The PS5 EMC (Embedded Micro Controller) uses hardware revision CXD90061GG with software versions ranging from 0.7.6 (prototype) to 1.14.3 (FW 9.20). EMC version can be extracted from serial flash at offset 0x4000 (0x7E000 bytes) using blsunpack.
## Key Concepts
- CXD90061GG hardware revision
- EMC v0.7.6: SDK 0.85.070 (prototype/DevKit)
- EMC v1.0.4: FW 1.01-1.14 (TestKit, Retail)
- EMC v1.2.3: FW 2.XX (TestKit)
- EMC v1.4.2: FW 3.00 (Retail)
- EMC v1.6.0: FW 4.00 (TestKit)
- EMC v1.8.2: FW 5.00 (Retail)
- EMC v1.8.3: FW 5.50 (Retail)
- EMC v1.14.3: FW 9.20 (Retail)
- Extracted from serial flash offset 0x4000, length 0x7E000
- SLB2 segment extraction via blsunpack
- C0080001 file contains EMC version
## System Role
Embedded controller firmware for power-on initialization

title: Error Codes
content_raw:
# Error Codes
## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Error_Codes
## System Layer
System Software / Error Handling
## Summary
Comprehensive list of PS5 error codes organized by prefix category, including CE (general), E2 (external), NP (network server), NW (network library), SU (software update), WS (web API server), and WV (web view). Each error code includes a description and recommended resolution steps.
## Key Concepts
- **CE-1xxxxx-x**: General/other errors (application, storage, disc, camera, data transfer, license, system update)
- **E2-xxxxxxxx**: External 2 errors (network connection)
- **NP-1xxxxx-x**: Network server errors (sign-in, entitlement, age restrictions, license confirmation)
- **NW-1xxxxx-x**: Network library errors (PlayStation Network connection)
- **SU-xxxxx-x**: Software update errors
- **WS-xxxxx-x**: Web API server errors
- **WV-xxxxx-x**: Web view errors
## System Role
Standardized error reporting and user guidance system for troubleshooting PS5 operational issues.

title: etaHEN: Primary All-in-One PS5 Homebrew Enabler
content_raw:
# etaHEN: Primary All-in-One PS5 Homebrew Enabler

## Source URL
https://github.com/etaHEN/etaHEN

## Domain
github.com

## System Layer
jailbreaking

## Summary
etaHEN by LightningMods is the primary all-in-one homebrew enabler for PS5, supporting FW 3.00-10.01 (latest version 2.4B). Replaces Debug Settings with a custom Toolbox UI and provides Rest Mode, Remote Play, Plugin/Payload ELF Menu, and External HDD Menu. Integrates Kstuff for fself/fpkg support, ItemzFlow for backup management, a game overlay (CPU/GPU temp, utilization, local IP), Direct PKG Installer v2 with WebUI on port 12800, FTP server on port 1337, Klog server on 9081, and ELF loader on port 9021. Includes game dumping, cheats/patches via Illusion plugin, Discord RPC, and controller shortcuts.

## Key Concepts
- Homebrew enabler replacing Debug Settings with Toolbox UI
- FW support: 3.00-10.01
- Kstuff integration for fself/fpkg loading
- ItemzFlow backup management integration
- Game Overlay: CPU/GPU temperature, utilization, local IP
- Direct PKG Installer v2 with WebUI (port 12800)
- FTP server (port 1337), Klog server (port 9081)
- ELF loader on port 9021 (john-tornblom's elfldr)
- etaHEN SDK for custom plugin extensions
- Illusion plugin for cheats and patches
- Discord RPC server (port 8000)
- Controller shortcut bindings
- Configurable via config.ini in /data/etaHEN

## Security Relevance
Operates as a post-exploitation framework that transforms a compromised kernel into a usable homebrew environment. By replacing the Sony Debug Settings with a custom Toolbox, it subverts Sony's code execution policy enforcement. The plugin SDK extends the attack surface by allowing third-party code to run with kernel-level privileges, bypassing all platform security controls.

## Relevance Tags
ps5, homebrew, enabler, payload, elf-loader, kstuff, fpkg, plugin, toolbox, lightningmods

title: Exploit Chains
content_raw:
# Exploit Chains
## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Exploit_Chains
## System Layer
system_software
## Summary
Comprehensive table of PS5 exploit chains organized by firmware version, hardware requirements, and capabilities. Covers chains from FW 1.00 through 10.01 combining hypervisor exploits (flatz's, Byepervisor), kernel exploits (umtx UaF, AIO Double Free, IPV6 UaF, exFAT), and usermode exploits (WebKit, BD-JB, Lua game savedata, mast1c0re).
## Key Concepts
- <=2.70 chains: flatz's hypervisor + umtx UaF (unreleased); Byepervisor + AIO/umtx (released by Specter)
- 1.00-5.50 chains: PSFree + AIO/umtx, WebKit loadInSameDocument
- 3.00-4.51 chains: IPV6_2292PKTOPTIONS UaF (CVE-2020-7457) + WebKit or BD-JB
- BD-JB chains (1.00-7.61): multiple combos with AIO/umtx + GPU DMA copy for >=6.00
- Lua game savedata chains (1.00-10.01): via hijacking Lua interpreter or mast1c0re
- <=4.03 exFAT driver heap exploit: kernel panic PoC only, no implementation
- Hypervisor exploit required for full HEN capabilities
- GPU DMA copy bypasses kernel .data write protection on FW >=6.00
- Capabilities: kstuff, usermode ELF loader (<=4.51), usermode ROP, native BD-J execution
## System Role
Exploit chain compatibility reference

title: Firmware Changelog
content_raw:
# Firmware Changelog

## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Firmware_Changelog

## System Layer
System Software

## Summary
Changelog for PS5 system software updates, covering versions from 20.02-02.25.00 (Nov 2020) through 21.01-05.10.00 (May 2022).

## Key Concepts

### Notable Changes
- **02.25.00**: Initial post-launch update; improved system performance, fixed download queue issue
- **02.26.00**: Fixed disc game deletion bug, fixed DualSense charging via front USB-A in rest mode
- **02.30.00**: Fixed data transfer/download cancellation, PS4 text input errors, Wi-Fi stability
- **02.50.00**: Fixed PS4 disc auto-install after PS5 upgrade; added Share Factory Studio clip editing
- **03.00.00**: Full changelog available externally
- **03.20.00**: DualSense firmware update, screen reader fixes, hidden game fix, USB storage copy stability
- **04.00.00**, **05.00.00**: Full changelogs available externally

## System Role
Provides a detailed history of PS5 firmware changes for tracking when specific features and bug fixes were introduced.

title: gbatemp PS5 Exploit Guide
content_raw:
# gbatemp PS5 Exploit Guide

## Source URL
https://gbatemp.net/threads/ps5-exploit-guide.613891/

## Domain
gbatemp.net

## System Layer
jailbreaking

## Summary
Community-maintained PS5 exploit guide by KiiWii (Ben Sellwood), last updated June 2026. Documents firmware version ranges from 2.XX through 13.XX with per-firmware exploit status, kernel exploit compatibility, userland entry points, hypervisor exploit availability, and payload tooling. Latest OFW is 13.40 as of June 2026.

## Key Concepts
- FW 2.XX supports KEX+HV via WebKit (2.50 best, 2.7X max)
- FW 3.XX-5.XX supports KEX+HV+Linux (5.50 best+max)
- FW 6.XX supports KEX only (6.02 max for Linux)
- FW 7.XX supports KEX only, no HV (7.61 best+max)
- FW 8.XX-12.70 supports KEX only (PS4/PS5 backups, no HV)
- FW 13.XX supports userland only, no KEX
- Kernel exploits: UMTX2 (1.00-7.61), Lapse (1.00-10.01), Poopsploit (4.03-12.00), P2JB (9.00-12.70)
- Userland entry points: LUA (2.00-LATEST), Y2JB (4.03-13.40), NFNH (4.03-12.XX), YARPE (4.03-12.XX), BD-JB (1.00-7.61), WebKit (1.00-5.50), LuaC0re (1.00-12.70)
- Hypervisor exploits confirmed up to 6.02, theoretical up to 7.XX
- Tools: Kstuff Lite, PS5 App Dumper, etaHEN, ItemzFlow, PS5 Debug NG
- Block Sony update servers via DNS to prevent forced OFW updates

## Security Relevance
Documents the complete trusted boot chain compromise model for PS5: userland entry points break application sandbox isolation, kernel exploits break OS kernel integrity, and hypervisor exploits break VM-level isolation. Each layer corresponds to a progressively deeper violation of the PS5 trusted execution environment.

## Relevance Tags
ps5, jailbreak, exploit, guide, firmware, kernel, hypervisor, userland, homebrew, payload

title: Google Cloud Confidential Computing
content_raw:
# Google Cloud Confidential Computing

## Source URL
https://cloud.google.com/confidential-computing

## Domain
cloud.google.com

## System Layer (choose one)
system_design

## Summary
Google Cloud's confidential computing portfolio providing encryption of data in use during processing. Includes Confidential VMs (using AMD SEV, Intel TDX), Confidential GKE Nodes, Confidential Space for multi-party collaboration, and Confidential Dataflow/Dataproc. Supports lift-and-shift migration with no code changes required.

## Key Concepts
- Confidential VMs with AMD SEV and Intel TDX for hardware-enforced memory encryption
- Confidential GKE Nodes for encrypted Kubernetes workload isolation
- Confidential Space for multi-party confidential collaboration and computation
- Confidential Dataflow and Dataproc for encrypted data pipeline processing
- Split-trust Encryption Tool for unified control of data at rest, in use, and in transit
- Shielded VM integration for rootkit and bootkit protection
- Attestation verification via Cloud Monitoring

## Security Relevance
Illustrates commercial deployment of hardware TEE technology (AMD SEV, Intel TDX) for protecting data in use. Confidential Space demonstrates trusted execution for multi-party computation, reducing trust in the cloud provider itself—a key pattern in zero-trust and confidential computing models.

## Relevance Tags
google cloud, confidential computing, confidential vm, amd sev, intel tdx, trusted execution environment, data in use, confidential space

title: GPU DMA Exploitation Internals: Architecture, Mechanism, and Implications
content_raw:
# GPU DMA Exploitation Internals: Architecture, Mechanism, and Implications

## Overview

The GPU DMA copy exploit is one of the most powerful kernel exploitation primitives on PS5, yet it remains one of the least documented. Since FW 6.00, the hypervisor enforces write protection on the kernel .data section — any CPU-originated write to kernel .data triggers an NPT violation and kernel panic. However, the GPU's DMA engine can bypass this protection, reading and writing kernel .data pages through crafted GPU command buffer submissions. This makes GPU DMA the primary mechanism for post-exploitation tasks: enabling Debug Settings, patching security flags, modifying credentials, and (potentially) reading XOM-protected kernel .text.

The exploit is NOT a vulnerability in the traditional sense — it is an architectural gap created by the separation between NPT-based CPU write protection and IOMMU-based DMA protection. The GPU communicates through libSceGnmDriver (the GNM driver), which constructs PM4 command packets submitted to the GPU's command processor. By crafting PM4 commands that embed kernel .data target addresses, an attacker with initial kernel read/write (via kqueueex, netcontrol, or aio_multi_delete) can write to kernel .data without triggering the hypervisor's write protection.

## GNM Driver and PM4 Command Architecture

### libSceGnmDriver

The GNM (Graphics Nifty Manager) driver is Sony's low-level graphics API for the PlayStation platform. On PS5, `libSceGnmDriver` (or `libSceGnmDriverForNeoMode.sprx` for PS4-compat mode) provides the userland-to-GPU interface:

**Key functions:**
- `sceGnmSubmitCommandBuffers` — Submit draw/compute command buffers to GPU
- `sceGnmSubmitAndFlipCommandBuffers` — Submit commands and flip display buffer
- `sceGnmDingDong` — Doorbell mechanism for async compute queues
- `sceGnmSubmitDone` — Signal command buffer submission completion
- `sceGnmAddEqEvent` — Register kernel event for GPU interrupts (EOP, ComputeXRelMem)

**Command buffer types:**
| Type | Name | Purpose | Processing Engine |
|------|------|---------|-------------------|
| DCB | Draw Command Buffer | Graphics rendering, state changes | ProcessGraphics |
| CCB | Constant Command Buffer | Pre-computed data, constant engine update | ProcessCeUpdate |
| ACB | Async Compute Buffer | Independent compute dispatches | ProcessCompute |

### PM4 Packet Format

PM4 (Packet Manager 4) is AMD's binary command format for GPU communication. Each packet has:

```
[PM4 Header (4 bytes)] [Payload (variable)]
```

**Header format:**
- Bits 31-30: Packet type (0 = regular, 1 = indirect buffer, 2 = chain, 3 = NOP)
- Bits 29-16: Payload size in dwords (0 = variable size based on opcode)
- Bits 15-0: Opcode

**Relevant PM4 opcodes for DMA exploitation:**
- `WRITE_DATA` — Write arbitrary data to a target address (key for DMA write primitive)
- `DMA_DATA` — DMA copy from source to destination (key for DMA copy primitive)
- `INDIRECT_BUFFER` — Chain to another command buffer
- `ACQUIRE_MEM` — Memory synchronization
- `RELEASE_MEM` — Memory synchronization with write acknowledgement
- `EVENT_WRITE` — Write event timestamp to memory
- `SET_PREDICATION` — Conditional execution control

### GPU DMA Engine

The GPU contains a dedicated DMA engine that can perform asynchronous memory copies independently of the graphics pipeline:

- **Source:** Any GPU-accessible memory (GDDR6, system RAM accessible via IOMMU)
- **Destination:** Any GPU-accessible memory
- **Operation:** Parallel copy with configurable source/destination stride
- **Synchronization:** Can signal completion via EOP (End of Pipe) interrupt events

The DMA engine operates through PM4 commands embedded in command buffers. When the GPU command processor encounters a `DMA_DATA` or `WRITE_DATA` packet, it programs the DMA engine to perform the memory operation. The key insight: the DMA engine accesses physical memory through the IOMMU, NOT through the CPU's NPT page tables.

## GPU DMA Exploit Chain

### Prerequisites

GPU DMA alone is NOT sufficient for exploitation — it requires:

1. **Kernel read primitive** (from kqueueex, netcontrol, aio_multi_delete, or umtx_shm)
   - Needed to leak kernel .data base virtual address
   - Needed to translate virtual addresses to physical addresses (kASLR defeat)
   - Optional: some implementations use kernel R/W for setup and GPU DMA only for writes
2. **GPU command buffer access** (through libSceGnmDriver)
   - Available from userland processes with graphics API access
   - BD-JB: can call `java.sun.awt.GnmUtils.copyPlanesBackgroundToPrimary`
   - Game savedata exploit: can call `sceGnmSubmitCommandBuffers` from `libSceGnmDriverForNeoMode.sprx`
3. **Allocated GPU buffer** containing crafted PM4 commands

### Chain Steps

```
1. Kernel exploit (kqueueex/netcontrol/aio) → kernel R/W primitive
2. Leak kernel .data base address (via kASLR defeat)
3. Translate virtual address → physical address (for GPU DMA target)
4. Craft PM4 command buffer with WRITE_DATA/DMA_DATA targeting kernel .data physical address
5. Submit crafted command buffer via libSceGnmDriver (sceGnmSubmitCommandBuffers)
6. GPU processes command → DMA engine writes to kernel .data
7. Hypervisor NPT write protection NOT triggered (DMA bypasses CPU page table walk)
8. Kernel .data modified: security_flags, target_id, qa_flags, utoken_flags patched
9. Debug Settings menu enabled
```

### What Gets Patched

The GPU DMA write targets specific fields in kernel .data:

| Field | Purpose | Effect |
|-------|---------|--------|
| `security_flags` | System security policy flags | Disables security enforcement |
| `target_id` | Console type identifier (Retail/DEX/CEX) | May enable debug capabilities |
| `qa_flags` | Quality assurance flags | Enables test/debug modes |
| `utoken_flags` | User token flags | Modifies authentication state |

The exact offsets vary by firmware version and must be determined through kernel binary analysis for each target FW.

### Usage Across Exploit Chains

| Exploit Chain | Userland Entry | Kernel Exploit | GPU DMA Role |
|---------------|---------------|----------------|--------------|
| BD-JB + umtx + GPU DMA | BD-JB (<=7.61) | umtx_shm UaF | Debug Settings + security flags |
| Y2JB + P2JB + GPU DMA | Y2JB (4.03-13.40) | kqueueex cr_ref | Automated in P2JB-Y2JB port 2.4 |
| Netcontrol/POOPS + GPU DMA | Lua/Y2JB/BD-J | netcontrol double fdrop | Security flag/QA flag/UTOKEN patching |
| Lapse + GPU DMA | Lua/Y2JB | aio_multi_delete | Post-exploitation patching |

Flatz's BD-JB implementation was the first to chain GPU DMA with umtx UaF (2025-01-05). hammer-83 followed with Java+UMTX+BD-JB (2025-02-08). Znullptr implemented a Lua chain with UMTX and Artemis games (2025-02-12). The P2JB-Y2JB port (matem6, May 2026) automated GPU DMA inside the JavaScript exploit, making Debug Settings activation transparent.

## Why GPU DMA Bypasses Hypervisor Write Protection

### NPT vs IOMMU

The fundamental architectural gap:

**NPT (Nested Page Tables)** — CPU-originated memory accesses:
- Walked on every CPU memory access (instruction fetch, load, store)
- Enforced at 4 KB granularity
- Managed by hypervisor
- **Trap:** Write to NPT-protected page causes VMEXIT_NPT_VIOLATION → hypervisor panics kernel
- **Applies to:** All CPU reads/writes (kernel mode, user mode)

**IOMMU (I/O Memory Management Unit)** — Device-originated memory accesses:
- Walked on every DMA transaction from PCIe devices
- Configurable page sizes (4 KB, 2 MB, 1 GB)
- Managed by hypervisor through hypercalls
- **Trap:** DMA to IOMMU-protected page causes IOMMU page fault → logged in event log (may or may not cause system action)
- **Applies to:** All DMA-capable devices (GPU, NVMe, USB, networking)

**The gap:** The hypervisor enforces different protection mechanisms for CPU vs DMA access. A page that is write-protected in the NPT (kernel .data) may NOT be write-protected in the IOMMU page tables. The GPU DMA engine accesses memory through the IOMMU, not the NPT, so it operates under different permissions.

### Why the IOMMU Does Not Block It

The most likely explanation involves one or more of:

1. **Kernel .data lacks IOMMU write-protection entries:** The IOMMU page tables simply do not have entries that restrict DMA writes to kernel .data pages. The IOMMU might use a default identity mapping for physical addresses that aren't explicitly assigned to guest buffers.

2. **GPU IOMMU domain grants broad access:** The GPU's IOMMU domain may be configured with wide memory access for performance (GPU needs to access framebuffer, textures, shaders, and system memory). Kernel .data happens to fall within this permitted range.

3. **PM4 command address validation bypass:** The GPU command processor validates PM4 command addresses at the IOMMU level, but the validation may not check whether the target address is kernel .data. The WRITE_DATA command may accept any address that falls within the GPU's IOMMU domain.

4. **Physical address aliasing:** If the GPU uses physical addresses directly (bypassing virtual translation), and kernel .data's physical address is within the GPU-accessible physical address range, the IOMMU has no way to distinguish "legitimate GPU buffer access" from "kernel .data access" at the same physical address.

### Why It's Unpatchable (Design Gap)

The GPU DMA bypass is fundamentally a design gap, not a software vulnerability:

- **Fix Option 1:** Add kernel .data to the IOMMU write-protection table
  - Requires the hypervisor to maintain IOMMU protection for kernel pages
  - Feasible but needs comprehensive IOMMU page table updates
  - May have performance implications for GPU-to-system-memory transfers

- **Fix Option 2:** Validate all GPU PM4 command target addresses against an allowed list
  - Requires kernel driver changes to libSceGnmDriver
  - Would break legitimate GPU-to-system-memory DMA patterns
  - Performance impact on graphics operations

- **Fix Option 3:** Route all GPU DMA through the NPT (eliminate IOMMU bypass)
  - Would require architectural changes to how GPU memory access works
  - Potentially very high engineering cost

- **Fix Option 4:** Disable GPU DMA writes to non-GPU-buffer regions entirely
  - Most likely effective fix
  - Would close the bypass at the IOMMU configuration level
  - Unknown if applied in any firmware version

## Exploit Delivery Methods

### BD-JB Path (Flatz, hammer-83)
```
BD-J exploit → umtx_shm UaF → kernel R/W → GPU DMA via GnmUtils.copyPlanesBackgroundToPrimary()
```
Advantage: `GnmUtils.copyPlanesBackgroundToPrimary()` provides a clean API for GPU DMA without needing to craft low-level PM4 commands. The BD-J Java runtime provides access to `java.sun.awt.GnmUtils` which is normally available to games.

### Game Savedata Path (Znullptr)
```
Artemis Engine Lua exploit → umtx_shm UaF → kernel R/W → GPU DMA via sceGnmSubmitCommandBuffers()
```
Requires: Crafting PM4 command buffer in Lua, submitting through libSceGnmDriverForNeoMode.sprx.

### Y2JB Path (matem6, P2JB-Y2JB port 2.4)
```
Y2JB V8 exploit → P2JB kqueueex → kernel R/W → GPU DMA automated in exploit JS
```
Most automated: GPU DMA is triggered automatically after P2JB completes, no separate step needed.

## Open Questions

### Mechanism
- Q-CRIT-009: Exact IOMMU configuration that allows GPU writes to kernel .data
- Q-CRIT-010: Can GPU DMA read kernel .text (XOM bypass)? Currently believed impossible but unconfirmed.
- Q-IMP-016: Complete PM4 command format for WRITE_DATA and DMA_DATA on PS5's Liverpool GPU

### Firmware Variation
- Q-IMP-017: Whether FW 13.40+ has changed GPU DMA behavior (hardened or closed the gap)
- Q-IMP-018: Whether different PS5 models (FAT, Slim, Pro) have variant GPU DMA behavior
- Q-MIN-004: Whether the bypass exists on PS4 or is PS5-specific (related to hypervisor introduction)

### Detection
- Q-IMP-019: Whether the hypervisor can detect GPU DMA writes to kernel .data (IOMMU event log monitoring)
- Q-IMP-020: Whether the southbridge EMC can detect anomalous DMA patterns
- Q-MIN-005: Whether GPU DMA reads to kernel .data leave detectable traces in GPU performance counters

### Exploit Potential
- Q-CRIT-021: Whether non-GPU DMA devices (NVMe SSD, USB, networking) have similar kernel .data access bypasses
- Q-IMP-022: Whether GPU DMA can be used as a standalone exploitation primitive (without prior kernel R/W)
- Q-IMP-023: Whether the GPU DMA engine can access hypervisor memory (would bypass hypervisor entirely)

## References

### PSDevWiki
- https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Vulnerabilities (GPU DMA copy section)
- https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Exploit_Chains
- https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Hypervisor (IOMMU management)

### GPU Architecture
- AMD RDNA 2 Instruction Set Architecture (public references)
- libSceGnmDriver reverse engineering (community sources)
- shadPS4 emulator GNM driver implementation (https://github.com/shadps4-emu/shadPS4)

### Related Research Files
- [[iommu_architecture]] — full IOMMU architecture on PS5, hypercall interface, event log
- [[hypervisor]] — NPT mechanism, xotext, CR filtering (why CPU writes are blocked)
- [[kernel]] — kernel .data section, security flags, kASLR
- [[hardware_attack_surface]] — GDDR6 shared memory, GPU architecture
- [[attack_surface]] — GPU DMA as entry point ranking #7
- [[mitigation_assessment]] — IOMMU/SMMU effectiveness, XOM assessment

2. **GPU IOMMU domain grants broad access:** The GPU's IOMMU domain may be configured with wide memory access for performance (GPU needs to access framebuffer, textures, shaders, and system memory). Kernel .data happens to fall within this permitted range.

3. **PM4 command address validation bypass:** The GPU command processor validates PM4 command addresses at the IOMMU level, but the validation may not check whether the target address is kernel .data. The WRITE_DATA command may accept any address that falls within the GPU's IOMMU domain.

4. **Physical address aliasing:** If the GPU uses physical addresses directly (bypassing virtual translation), and kernel .data's physical address is within the GPU-accessible physical address range, the IOMMU has no way to distinguish "legitimate GPU buffer access" from "kernel .data access" at the same physical address.

### Why It's Unpatchable (Design Gap)

The GPU DMA bypass is fundamentally a design gap, not a software vulnerability:

- **Fix Option 1:** Add kernel .data to the IOMMU write-protection table
 - Requires the hypervisor to maintain IOMMU protection for kernel pages
 - Feasible but needs comprehensive IOMMU page table updates
 - May have performance implications for GPU-to-system-memory transfers

- **Fix Option 2:** Validate all GPU PM4 command target addresses against an allowed list
 - Requires kernel driver changes to libSceGnmDriver
 - Would break legitimate GPU-to-system-memory DMA patterns
 - Performance impact on graphics operations

- **Fix Option 3:** Route all GPU DMA through the NPT (eliminate IOMMU bypass)
 - Would require architectural changes to how GPU memory access works
 - Potentially very high engineering cost

- **Fix Option 4:** Disable GPU DMA writes to non-GPU-buffer regions entirely
 - Most likely effective fix
 - Would close the bypass at the IOMMU configuration level
 - Unknown if applied in any firmware version

## Exploit Delivery Methods

### BD-JB Path (Flatz, hammer-83)
```
BD-J exploit → umtx_shm UaF → kernel R/W → GPU DMA via GnmUtils.copyPlanesBackgroundToPrimary()
```
Advantage: `GnmUtils.copyPlanesBackgroundToPrimary()` provides a clean API for GPU DMA without needing to craft low-level PM4 commands. The BD-J Java runtime provides access to `java.sun.awt.GnmUtils` which is normally available to games.

### Game Savedata Path (Znullptr)
```
Artemis Engine Lua exploit → umtx_shm UaF → kernel R/W → GPU DMA via sceGnmSubmitCommandBuffers()
```
Requires: Crafting PM4 command buffer in Lua, submitting through libSceGnmDriverForNeoMode.sprx.

### Y2JB Path (matem6, P2JB-Y2JB port 2.4)
```
Y2JB V8 exploit → P2JB kqueueex → kernel R/W → GPU DMA automated in exploit JS
```
Most automated: GPU DMA is triggered automatically after P2JB completes, no separate step needed.

## Open Questions

### Mechanism
- Q-CRIT-009: Exact IOMMU configuration that allows GPU writes to kernel .data
- Q-CRIT-010: Can GPU DMA read kernel .text (XOM bypass)? Currently believed impossible but unconfirmed.
- Q-IMP-016: Complete PM4 command format for WRITE_DATA and DMA_DATA on PS5's Liverpool GPU

### Firmware Variation
- Q-IMP-017: Whether FW 13.40+ has changed GPU DMA behavior (hardened or closed the gap)
- Q-IMP-018: Whether different PS5 models (FAT, Slim, Pro) have variant GPU DMA behavior
- Q-MIN-004: Whether the bypass exists on PS4 or is PS5-specific (related to hypervisor introduction)

### Detection
- Q-IMP-019: Whether the hypervisor can detect GPU DMA writes to kernel .data (IOMMU event log monitoring)
- Q-IMP-020: Whether the southbridge EMC can detect anomalous DMA patterns
- Q-MIN-005: Whether GPU DMA reads to kernel .data leave detectable traces in GPU performance counters

### Exploit Potential
- Q-CRIT-021: Whether non-GPU DMA devices (NVMe SSD, USB, networking) have similar kernel .data access bypasses
- Q-IMP-022: Whether GPU DMA can be used as a standalone exploitation primitive (without prior kernel R/W)
- Q-IMP-023: Whether the GPU DMA engine can access hypervisor memory (would bypass hypervisor entirely)

## References

### PSDevWiki
- https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Vulnerabilities (GPU DMA copy section)
- https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Exploit_Chains
- https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Hypervisor (IOMMU management)

### GPU Architecture
- AMD RDNA 2 Instruction Set Architecture (public references)
- libSceGnmDriver reverse engineering (community sources)
- shadPS4 emulator GNM driver implementation (https://github.com/shadps4-emu/shadPS4)

### Related Research Files
- [[iommu_architecture]] — full IOMMU architecture on PS5, hypercall interface, event log
- [[hypervisor]] — NPT mechanism, xotext, CR filtering (why CPU writes are blocked)
- [[kernel]] — kernel .data section, security flags, kASLR
- [[hardware_attack_surface]] — GDDR6 shared memory, GPU architecture
- [[attack_surface]] — GPU DMA as entry point ranking #7
- [[mitigation_assessment]] — IOMMU/SMMU effectiveness, XOM assessment

title: Hardware
content_raw:
# Hardware
## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Hardware
## System Layer
hardware
## Summary
Comprehensive hardware specifications for PS5 standard and digital editions. Covers CPU, GPU, memory, storage, I/O, wireless, power supply, dimensions, and pricing by region.
## Key Concepts
- CPU: 8-core/16-thread x86-64 AMD Ryzen Zen 2, variable frequency up to 3.5 GHz
- GPU: AMD Radeon RDNA 2, Ray Tracing Acceleration, variable frequency up to 2.23 GHz
- Floating Point Performance: 10.28 TFLOPS
- Memory: 16 GB GDDR6, 256-bit, 448 GB/s bandwidth
- Storage: Custom 825 GB SSD, PCIe 4.0, 5.5 GB/s raw read bandwidth
- External Storage: USB HDD support
- Audio: Tempest 3D Audio Tech
- I/O: Gigabit Ethernet, 3x USB 3.1, 1x USB-C, HDMI 2.1
- Wireless: Wi-Fi IEEE 802.11ax, Bluetooth 5.1
- Video Output: 4K 120Hz, VRR
- Disc Drive: 4K Ultra HD Blu-ray (standard only)
- Power Supply: 350W (standard), 340W (digital)
- Dimensions: 390x260x104mm (standard), 390x260x92mm (digital)
- Weight: 4.5 kg (standard), 3.9 kg (digital)
- Controller: DualSense
## System Role
Core hardware specification reference

title: Homebrew Enabler
content_raw:
# Homebrew Enabler
## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Homebrew_Enabler
## System Layer
system_software
## Summary
Catalog of PS5 homebrew enablers organized by exploit type. HEN via hypervisor exploit (Prosperous HEN FW 1.00-4.51, Byepervisor HEN FW 1.00-2.70), via kernel exploit (HEN-V FW 3.00-4.51, etaHEN FW 2.00-10.01), via JIT code execution (Mast1c0re part 2 FW 2.00-13.00), and partial HEN solutions (kstuff, libhijacker, BackPork, PS4 EBOOT DLC Patcher). PS5 has both kernel and hypervisor securities making HEN harder and less powerful than PS4.
## Key Concepts
- Prosperous HEN: chains TMR vulnerability (fail0verflow) with Byepervisor HEN, FW 1.00-4.51
- Byepervisor HEN: FW 1.00-2.70, via Specter's hypervisor exploit
- HEN-V: kernel-only HEN, FW 3.00-4.51 (astrelsky)
- etaHEN: kernel-only HEN, FW 2.00-10.01 (LightningMods)
- Mast1c0re part 2: JIT execution via PS2 Classics emulator, FW 2.00-13.00, requires Star Wars: Racer Revenge
- kstuff: payload for kernel ELF loading, FW 3.00-12.00
- kstuff-toggle: disables kstuff after game launch for performance
- libhijacker: library injection, FW 3.00-4.51
- BackPork: background auto-sideload payload
- PS4 EBOOT DLC Patcher: PC tool for DLC unlocking
## System Role
Homebrew enablement method reference

title: Hypervisor
content_raw:
# Hypervisor
## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Hypervisor
## System Layer
hypervisor
## Summary
PS5 uses a custom hypervisor (HV) for Virtualization-Based Security (VBS), protecting kernel integrity through nested paging (SLAT/NPT), control register filtering, MSR protection, and execute-only memory (xotext). On FW ≤ 2.70 the hypervisor is integrated into the kernel binary; later versions have it as a separately loaded component.
## Key Concepts
- Custom hypervisor (not based on existing projects)
- Virtualization-Based Security (VBS) for kernel integrity protection
- Separate loading: in-kernel (≤ 2.70) vs standalone (> 2.70)
- 17 hypercalls (vmmcalls: 0x00-0x10)
- VMMCALL_HV_GET_MESSAGE_CONF (0), GET_MESSAGE_COUNT (1)
- VMMCALL_HV_START_LOADING_SELF (2), FINISH_LOADING_SELF (3)
- VMMCALL_HV_SET_CPUID_PS4 (4), SET_CPUID_PPR (5)
- VMMCALL_HV_IOMMU_SET_GUEST_BUFFERS (6)
- VMMCALL_HV_IOMMU_ENABLE_DEVICE (7)
- VMMCALL_HV_IOMMU_BIND_PASID (8), UNBIND_PASID (9)
- VMMCALL_HV_IOMMU_CHECK_CMD_COMPLETION (10)
- VMMCALL_HV_IOMMU_CHECK_EVLOG_REGS (11)
- VMMCALL_HV_IOMMU_READ_DEVICE_TABLE (12)
- VMMCALL_HV_GET_TMR_VIOLATION_ERROR (13)
- VMMCALL_HV_VMCLOSURE_INVOCATION (14, FW ≥ 3.00)
- VMMCALL_HV_STARTUP_MP (15, FW ≥ 3.00)
- VMMCALL_HV_DISABLE_STARTUP_MP (16, FW ≥ 3.00)
- AMD Secure Virtual Machine (SVM) features used: NPT, GMET, CR/MSR interception
- Nested Page Tables (SLAT) for second-level address translation
- Guest Mode Execute Trap (GMET) prevents code execution from wrong privilege level
- xotext (execute-only memory, bit 58 in NPT PTEs) - hardware-backed via AMD collaboration
- Filtered CR0 bits: PG (31), WP (16), NE (5), PE (0)
- Filtered CR4 bits: SMAP (21), SMEP (20), VME (0)
- Masked EFER bits: nda/xotext (16), SVME (12), NXE (11)
- MSR Protection Map (MSRPM) bitmap for protected MSRs
- VMEXIT_CPUID handled (PS4 emulation)
- VMEXIT_RDPRU injected with #GP
- IOMMU management moved from kernel to hypervisor
- Only virtualizes kernel/usermode (GameOS), no multiple VMs
## System Role
Kernel integrity protection via hardware-assisted virtualization

title: Hypervisor Loader
content_raw:
# Hypervisor Loader

## Source URL
https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/Hypervisor_Loader

## System Layer
Hypervisor

## Summary
Introduced in FW 03.00.00.33 and above. Codename: "HyLonome".

## Key Concepts
- First appeared in FW 3.00
- Acts as an intermediate loader between the Secure Loader and the Hypervisor
- Codename suggests "Hypervisor Loader — No ME" or similar

## System Role
The Hypervisor Loader adds an additional stage to the PS5 boot chain (introduced in FW 3.00). This suggests Sony hardened the boot sequence by adding a verified loader stage between the IPL/Secure Loader and the Hypervisor itself.

title: IDU Mode
content_raw:
# IDU Mode

**Source:** https://www.psdevwiki.com/ps5/IDU_Mode
**System Layer:** System Software
**Summary:** IDU (Individual Display Unit) mode for kiosk/demo PS5 consoles.
**Key Concepts:** IDU Daemon, IDU Client, IDU Utility, Kiosk mode, Demo mode, Title IDs, sflash/NOR flag, PSN restriction
**System Role:** Specialized system mode for retail demo/kiosk consoles that restricts functionality and manages demo content delivery.

## Overview

IDU (Individual Display Unit) is a mode designed for Kiosk/Demo PS5 consoles meant to demonstrate different game demos, features, etc., usually found in game/electronic stores.

On the PS4, IDU Mode is automatically activated and all content is automatically transferred by inserting a PS4 IDU Disc. This could be the same situation for the PS5, however no PS5 IDU disc has been dumped as of today.

A Kiosk/Demo PS5 console that has used the PS5 IDU disc to install the IDU Utility Normal application has been found. The IDU Utility Normal is an application installed on PS5 internal storage with a size of 3.87 MB.

### Key Characteristics
- Similar to a game hub with quick launch demos, information, icons, and videos (trailers)
- When no bundle is installed, a splash screen with "Content unavailable" is displayed
- IDU mode restricts both application launching and PlayStation Network features
- Activating IDU Mode flashes a value to sflash/NOR that prevents removal even when reinstalling or updating firmware

### Setup/Settings
- Select Country or Region
- Select Language: Multiple languages (same ones PS5 supports)
- Select Region: Multiple regions (same ones PS5 supports)
- Disable Downloads: ON/OFF
- Allow Game Downloads: ON/OFF (controls automatic game demo downloads)
- Allow Video Downloads: ON/OFF (controls automatic trailer downloads)
- Disable Attract Mode Audio: ON/OFF
- Virtual Controller: ON/OFF

### Status Screen
- System Software Version: Current installed firmware
- System Hardware ID: Console's ID (not usually shown in Settings)
- Client Version: Console's IDU client version (similar to PS4's OMSK)
- Device Name: Device name, same as found in Settings
- Installed Bundle: Bundle version, could be found on the disc
- Bundle Name: Bundle name, could be the same name as the disc

## Internal Title IDs

10 different Title IDs referring to IDU have been found inside PS5's firmware:

| Title ID | Internal Name | Information |
|---|---|---|
| NPXS40069 | TITLEID_SIE_IDU_DAEMON | IDU Daemon - activates recognizing and installing retail packages (IDU demos) from server |
| NPXS45085 | TITLEID_SIE_IDU_CLIENT | IDU Client - main application providing GUI to the whole process |
| NPXS45181 | TITLEID_SIE_IDU_UTILITY_FULL | IDU Utility Full Operation switch |
| NPXS45182 | TITLEID_SIE_IDU_UTILITY_NORMAL | IDU Utility Normal Operation switch |
| NPXS21005 | TITLEID_SCE_DAEMON_IDU | IDU Daemon (same ID as PS4, possibly for backwards compatibility) |
| NPXS29005 | TITLEID_SCE_APP_IDU | IDU OMSK Client (same ID as PS4, possibly for backwards compatibility) |
| NPXS29800 | TITLEID_SCE_IDU_UTILITY | IDU Utility, possibly controllable only via disc |
| NPXS29801 | TITLEID_SCE_IDU_UTILITY_REGION | IDU Utility Region switch |
| PPSL07787 | TITLEID_SIE_IDU_CLIENT_FOR_E1 | IDU Client for E1 (purpose unknown) |
| PPSA01759 | TITLEID_SIE_IDU_CLIENT_FOR_PROD | IDU Client for Production (purpose unknown) |
